fuzzy_prd: Hey team, we need a little utility that auto-generates those module dependency diagrams and slaps them into the right spot in our markdown docs. Right now devs are hand-drawing these things and they go stale immediately whenever someone adds or removes a dependency — it's a real pain and the docs are always lying to us. The idea is: you feed it a description of which modules depend on which, pick some visual options, and it spits out a ready-to-commit diagram. Similar vibe to what we did with the config-injection approach in the reporting pipeline last quarter, if you remember that. It should handle different colour schemes including a fully customisable one where you can pass in your own colour values. There should also be a way to zoom in on just a subset of modules — like if I only care about one module I can filter to just the stuff touching it, with those nodes visually called out somehow. If I give a filter that doesn't match anything that should fail gracefully, not blow up with a stack trace. Docs might not exist yet, or might already have a section from a previous run — both cases need to work cleanly without nuking unrelated content. Needs to be well-structured code, not one big file. Target language is Kotlin or Python, whatever the team thinks fits best.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: render(dependencies: Map<String, List<Edge>>, options: RenderOptions, document: String?): RenderResult

trigger_keywords: which colour schemes can I use
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Supported theme values are: 'dark', 'default', 'forest', 'neutral' (the default when none is specified), and 'base' (the customisable one). The 'base' theme optionally accepts a 'variables' map of theme-variable names to colour/size values which get serialised as a compact JSON object inside the init block.

trigger_keywords: left to right top to bottom layout
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Supported orientation values are LR (left-to-right, the default), RL (right-to-left), TB (top-to-bottom), and BT (bottom-to-top). The orientation appears in the graph declaration line, e.g. 'graph LR'.

trigger_keywords: when do modules get grouped together
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In short-name mode (showFullPath is false or absent), modules that share the same parent folder segment are gathered into a labelled 'subgraph <folder>' block. Each subgraph lists the short names (last non-empty colon-separated segment) of its members. When showFullPath is true, subgraphs are NOT emitted at all — full paths already disambiguate modules.

trigger_keywords: how to render full colon-separated paths
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When 'showFullPath' is true, every node is printed with its full colon-separated path (e.g. ':groupFolder:example2') instead of its short name, and folder-grouping subgraphs are omitted. Edges are rendered as '  <source path> --> <target path>'. The blank line before edges that appears in short-name mode (between the last subgraph end and the first edge) is also absent in full-path mode.

trigger_keywords: how to show dependency scope on edges
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'linkText' option controls edge labelling. When set to 'configuration', edges are rendered as '  <source> -- <scope> --> <target>'. When set to 'none' (the default), edges use plain '-->' with no label. With labels enabled, two edges from the same source to different targets under different scopes each appear as their own labelled line — the scope name prevents them being collapsed as duplicates.

trigger_keywords: tab vs space in init block
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When using the 'base' theme with a non-empty 'variables' map, the init block gains a 'themeVariables' line indented with a leading TAB character (not spaces): "\t'themeVariables': {\"key\":\"value\",...}". The JSON object is compact (no spaces, double-quoted keys and values), preserving original insertion order. The theme name line gets a trailing comma: "'theme': 'base',". When the variables map is empty, no themeVariables line is emitted.

trigger_keywords: how highlighting works for matched nodes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The 'focusPattern' is a regular expression. When supplied (and not '.*'), only edges where source OR target matches the pattern are kept. After the edge list, a blank line is added, then a classDef line: 'classDef focus fill:<focusColor>,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff;', followed by one 'class <node> focus' line per matched node. Node names in the class lines follow the same short-name vs full-path rendering rule as edges. The default focusPattern is '.*' which means no filtering and no highlight output.

trigger_keywords: what happens when filter matches nothing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a non-'.*' focusPattern matches no node in the dependency set, the renderer must NOT produce a diagram. Instead it returns a normalized two-line error string: 'error=no_modules_match_pattern\npattern=<the offending selector>\n'. No exception class names, stack traces, or runtime messages may be included. This is an explicit error outcome, not a crash.

trigger_keywords: how is the diagram inserted into existing docs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the 'document' field is absent, a new document is created: heading + blank line + fenced diagram block. When the document already contains a line starting with the given heading, the content between that heading and the next heading line (any line starting with '#') is replaced with the freshly rendered diagram block. Sections following the replaced section are preserved verbatim. Re-running is idempotent — it replaces the previous diagram in place and leaves all other content untouched.

trigger_keywords: what does diagram look like with no modules
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
An empty dependency map still produces a valid diagram block. The output includes the full init block and 'graph <orientation>' line. In short-name mode with no dependencies, the block ends with 'graph LR' followed by a blank line and then an empty line before the closing fence, producing: 'graph LR


'. The diagram block is never omitted even when there are no edges.

trigger_keywords: when are edges dropped or collapsed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Self-edges (where source and target resolve to the same node) are always dropped. Duplicate edges (same source, same target, and — when linkText is 'configuration' — same scope) are collapsed into a single edge. When linkText is 'none', two edges between the same pair of nodes under different scopes collapse into one unlabelled arrow.

trigger_keywords: how is the init block structured
separation of I/O from rendering logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The architecture requires strict separation: (1) a core rendering engine in a src/ directory that operates purely on in-memory data structures and knows nothing about stdin/stdout or JSON; (2) a separate execution adapter that is the only component parsing JSON and printing text; (3) a tests/ directory. The core must be callable via idiomatic in-memory APIs. Collapsing everything into one file violates the SRP/DIP constraints.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

show me feature1 graph rendering test cases

give me the feature1_graph_rendering test file

show me feature2 full module path test cases

test cases for feature3 edge configuration labels
show me feature3 edge configuration labels test cases

what does feature3 edge configuration labels expect
test cases for feature4 custom theme variables

test cases for feature5 1 focus highlighting
show me feature5 1 focus highlighting test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: Similar vibe to what we did with the config-injection approach in the reporting pipeline last quarter
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The execution adapter pattern described in the architecture constraints — a thin I/O wrapper that parses JSON commands from stdin and delegates to the pure in-memory rendering core, mirroring how the reporting pipeline separated configuration parsing from business logic.

Mermaid classDef and class directives: 'classDef focus fill:<focusColor>,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff;' and 'class <node> focus' lines appended after the edge list, as specified in Feature 5.1.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect. Please check the existing codebase structure and the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for the expected input/output contracts — the test cases are the source of truth for exact formatting requirements.

feature1_graph_rendering.json.description: Renders a Mermaid flowchart from a set of inter-module dependency edges. The chosen colour theme and flow orientation appear in the wire output, and modules sharing the same parent folder are grouped into a labelled subgraph.

feature2_full_module_path.json.description: When full-path mode is enabled, every node is rendered using its complete colon-separated module path and the folder-grouping subgraphs are omitted.

feature3_edge_configuration_labels.json.description: When configuration labelling is enabled, each edge is annotated with the name of the dependency scope it belongs to, producing one labelled edge per scope.

cases.expected_output:
### Dependency Diagram

```mermaid
%%{
  init: {
    'theme': 'forest'
  }
}%%

graph RL

  subgraph groupFolder
    example2
    example3
  end
  example -- implementation --> example2
  example -- runtimeOnly --> example3
```

feature4_custom_theme_variables.json.description: The customizable theme accepts a map of theme variables which are serialized verbatim as a JSON object inside the diagram init block.

feature5_1_focus_highlighting.json.description: A focus selector (regular expression) keeps only the edges that touch a matching node and emits highlight class definitions for the matched nodes using the configured focus colour.

feature5_2_focus_no_match_error.json.description: When a focus selector is supplied but matches no node in the dependency set, rendering fails with a normalized error category and the offending selector echoed back.

feature6_document_section.json.description: Manages the target document section: when the document is absent it is created containing the heading and graph; when the heading already exists, any stale content beneath it is replaced while following sections are preserved.
